display_name: Atlantic Crossing — Irish Famine Coffin Ships

segments.from: Irish port of departure (Cork/Cobh, Liverpool, Galway, Dublin, Limerick)
segments.cost: £3-£5 (often subsidized by landlords conducting 'assisted emigration,' which was frequently forced eviction with a one-way ticket)
segments.conditions: Famine ships were often aging timber cargo vessels hastily retrofitted with wooden bunks to carry human cargo. Passengers boarded already weakened by months of starvation, and the cramped, unventilated holds became breeding grounds for typhus — carried by lice that thrived in the filthy, overcrowded conditions.

segments.to: North American port of arrival (Boston, New York, Quebec/Grosse Île, Saint John NB, Philadelphia)
segments.travel_time: 2-4 weeks (total crossing 4-8 weeks by sailing vessel)
segments.cost: included in passage; survivors arriving in Quebec often had to pay additional fares overland to reach US destinations
segments.conditions: Mortality rates of 20-30% were recorded on the worst voyages, with bodies buried at sea daily on some crossings. Ships arriving at Grosse Île quarantine station in the St. Lawrence sometimes had more dead than living passengers aboard, and the quarantine island itself became a mass grave for thousands who survived the crossing only to die within sight of land.

who_used_it: Irish famine refugees, overwhelmingly from the hardest-hit counties of western Ireland — Cork, Kerry, Clare, Galway, Mayo, Roscommon, Sligo, and Donegal. Most were Irish-speaking Catholic smallholders and cottiers who had been subsistence farmers on subdivided plots too small to survive a single crop failure. Landlords including Major Denis Mahon and Lord Palmerston organized mass eviction-and-emigration schemes.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} likely sailed from {origin} on one of the timber vessels that carried over a million famine refugees across the Atlantic, enduring 4 to 8 weeks in conditions so lethal that contemporaries called them coffin ships, before landing at {destination} with little more than survival itself.
source_references: Kerby A. Miller, 'Emigrants and Exiles: Ireland and the Irish Exodus to North America' (Oxford University Press, 1985)
Cormac Ó Gráda, 'Black '47 and Beyond: The Great Irish Famine in History, Economy, and Memory' (Princeton University Press, 1999)
Edward Laxton, 'The Famine Ships: The Irish Exodus to America' (Henry Holt, 1997)
Robert Whyte, 'The Ocean Plague: or, A Voyage to Quebec in an Irish Emigrant Vessel' (1848; reprinted by General Books, 2009)
